content:
Key Findings

  • South Ribble Borough Council spent a total of £178.0M across 18,517 transactions from 2021/22 to 2025/26
  • The council's spending patterns show significant variations throughout the financial year
  • March typically sees a spike in spending as the financial year ends
  • Weekday spending is concentrated on Mondays and Tuesdays
  • Seasonal variations include higher spending in the first quarter of each financial year

Year-End Spending Spikes

South Ribble Borough Council's spending data reveals a clear pattern of increased expenditure towards the end of each financial year. This is evident in the £178.0M total spent across 18,517 transactions from 2021/22 to 2025/26. The month of March, in particular, often sees a notable spike in spending as departments rush to utilise their remaining budgets before the financial year concludes.

This end-of-year surge is not unique to South Ribble but is a common phenomenon across local authorities. It reflects the pressure to spend allocated funds efficiently and effectively. However, it also raises questions about whether this timing optimises value for money or simply meets budgetary targets.

Weekday Spending Patterns

The data also highlights distinct patterns in the days of the week when spending occurs. Mondays and Tuesdays are particularly busy, accounting for a significant portion of the transactions. This could be due to the processing of invoices and payments that accumulate over the weekend. Conversely, Fridays tend to see lower spending volumes, possibly as staff prepare for the weekend.

Understanding these patterns can help the council manage its cash flow more effectively. By anticipating peaks and troughs in spending, financial officers can ensure that funds are available when needed and that suppliers are paid promptly. This can also help in negotiating better terms with suppliers, as consistent payment schedules can build stronger relationships.

Seasonal Variations

Seasonal variations in spending are also apparent in the data. The first quarter of each financial year, from April to June, often sees higher spending levels. This could be due to the start of new projects and initiatives that are funded at the beginning of the financial year. Additionally, the summer months may see increased expenditure on maintenance and repairs, particularly for outdoor facilities and infrastructure.

Conversely, the fourth quarter, from January to March, can be a period of heightened activity as departments aim to utilise their remaining budgets. This is consistent with the year-end spending spikes observed in the data. Understanding these seasonal trends can help the council plan its budget more effectively and ensure that funds are allocated where they are needed most.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to South Ribble Borough Council about the reasons behind the year-end spending spikes
  • Attend council committee meetings to discuss budget planning and spending patterns
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see how suppliers are selected and paid
  • Write to your local councillor about the impact of spending patterns on local services and value for money
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data and identify trends in your area
This article is based on South Ribble Borough Council's published spending data covering 2021/22 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
